claim_text: The outage mechanism began with a CI race condition and aggressive concurrency control that canceled a build, after which a later deploy treated artifacts as complete and pushed Lambda and ECS definitions pointing to a non-existent container ID.
mechanism_text: A CI race condition, amplified by aggressive concurrency control, erroneously canceled a build. A later deploy then falsely treated artifacts as complete and updated Lambda and ECS service definitions to reference a non-existent container ID, creating the broken deployment state that fed the later outage.
bounds: The source explicitly says the outage depended on three overlapping issues, so this supports a major causal path rather than a sole-cause account.

claim_text: Terraform’s rejection of the invalid Lambda update after ECS service definitions had already been mutated left ECS unable to start replacement tasks, leading restarted tasks to fail and ingest load balancers to return 503s.
mechanism_text: Terraform rejected the invalid Lambda change only after ECS definitions had already been mutated. That partial failure left ECS pointing at invalid definitions, so replacement tasks could not enter production; when tasks later restarted, they failed to come up and ingest load balancers began returning 503s.
bounds: The evidence supports the chain from partial mutation to restart failure to customer-facing 503s, but it does not quantify how much task churn was required before failure became effectively total.
